skill.name: skills-cli
skill.description: Use when users ask to discover, install, list, check, update, remove, back up, restore, sync, or initialize Agent Skills, mention `bunx skills`, `npx skills`, `skills.sh`, or `skills-lock.json`, ask "find a skill for X", or want help extending agent capabilities with installable skills.
skill.summary: Package manager for Agent Skills - discover, install, and manage reusable AI agent capabilities.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 113 patterns (external_commands: 98, network: 9, blocker: 6) but all are FALSE POSITIVES. The scanned file SKILL.md is documentation-only containing markdown code blocks and hyperlinks, not executable code. No actual shell execution, network calls, or cryptographic operations exist in this skill. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Install and manage AI agent skills with the CLI
content.value_statement: Finding and installing reusable AI capabilities is fragmented and tedious. The skills CLI provides a unified package manager to discover, install, and manage skills across Claude, Codex, and other agents.
actual_capabilities: Search and discover skills using bunx skills find with keyword queries
Install skills from GitHub, GitLab, or local paths with bunx skills add
List, check for updates, and update installed skills
Remove skills and restore from skills-lock.json manifest
Initialize new skill packages with bunx skills init
Sync skills across multiple AI agent directories
limitations: Requires Bun or Node.js runtime environment for CLI execution
Skills must be published in compatible SKILL.md format
Agent-specific flags required when targeting Codex or other non-Claude agents
Discovery depends on skills.sh indexer and community submissions

use_cases.title: Developer extending agent capabilities
use_cases.description: A developer wants to add code review and testing skills to their Claude setup. They use bunx skills find to discover relevant skills, verify quality metrics, and install with bunx skills add --skill pr-review -a claude.
use_cases.target_user: Software developers using AI coding assistants

use_cases.title: Team standardizing AI workflows
use_cases.description: A team lead wants consistent skills across team members. They use bunx skills ls --json to export current setup, then share a skills-lock.json for others to restore identical configurations.

use_cases.title: Power user discovering specialized skills
use_cases.description: An advanced user seeks domain-specific capabilities like PDF extraction or design review. They search the skills.sh catalog, filter by install count and publisher reputation, then install verified skills.
use_cases.target_user: AI power users and automation enthusiasts

prompt_templates.title: Find a skill for a specific task
prompt_templates.prompt: Find skills for automated testing in React projects. Search with relevant keywords and show me options with at least 1000 installs from trusted publishers.

prompt_templates.title: Install a skill to a specific agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Install the pr-review skill to my Codex agent using bunx skills add with the correct flags for Codex targeting and skip prompts.

prompt_templates.title: Audit and update existing skills
prompt_templates.prompt: Check all my currently installed skills for updates, list what changed, and update everything that has a newer version available.

prompt_templates.title: Back up and restore skills configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Export my current skills installation to a manifest file, then show me how another team member could restore this exact setup on their machine.

output_examples.input: Find skills for PDF text extraction
output: I found a skill that should fit.
Why it matches: specializes in extracting text from PDF documents with OCR support
Quality check: 15K installs, official publisher, well-maintained repository
Install: bunx skills add anthropics/skills --skill pdf-extract
Want me to install this for your Claude agent?

output_examples.input: List all skills installed for Codex
output: Use 'bunx skills check' to verify if updates are available

best_practices: Always verify skill quality before installing - check install count (prefer 1K+), publisher reputation, and repository stars
Use --skill flag to explicitly name skills and avoid installing entire packages unintentionally
Specify -a <agent> when targeting a specific AI agent to prevent scope confusion
Run bunx skills ls --json periodically to audit installed skills and track versions
Keep a skills-lock.json manifest for reproducible setups across machines or team members
anti_patterns: Installing skills without checking publisher reputation or install metrics
Using vague search terms like 'testing' instead of specific queries like 'react testing jest'
Giving up after one failed search - try alternative keywords or check skills.sh directly
Assuming all skills work with all agents - verify compatibility before installing

faq.question: What is the difference between bunx skills and npx skills?
faq.answer: They are the same CLI tool running through different package runners. Use bunx skills if you have Bun installed for faster execution, or npx skills if you only have Node.js. The commands and behavior are identical.

faq.question: How do I know if a skill is safe to install?
faq.answer: Check three signals: install count (prefer 1K+), publisher reputation (official sources like anthropics, openai, microsoft are trusted), and repository quality (100+ stars indicates community validation). Skills meeting all three criteria are generally safe.

faq.question: Can I use skills across Claude, Codex, and other agents?
faq.answer: Yes, many skills are agent-agnostic. Use the -a or --agent flag to specify your target agent during installation. Some skills may have agent-specific variants or requirements.

faq.question: What is the difference between symlinks and --copy during installation?
faq.answer: Symlinks (default) create references to the original skill files, keeping updates centralized. The --copy flag creates independent copies. Use symlinks for normal use; use --copy for CI environments, portability, or when symlinks are unsupported.

faq.question: How do I back up my skills setup for use on another machine?
faq.answer: Use bunx skills ls --json to export your configuration, or maintain a skills-lock.json file. For full backup and restore workflows across machines, consider using Skills Vault (bunx skvlt backup/restore).

faq.question: What should I do if bunx skills find returns no results?
faq.answer: Try alternative search terms, check skills.sh directly in a browser, or use broader category keywords. If no skill exists, you can create your own with bunx skills init to define a custom workflow.
